name: Pluggable Modes and Multi-Session
description: Pluggable domain modes, multi-session support, and a non-blocking question mechanism for the Ralph framework.

tasks.description: KEEP THIS SIMPLE. The task looks big but the model is tight. All existing perspective files in `framework/perspectives/` (architect, qa-engineer, design-reviewer, spec-reviewer, implementer, code-cleaner, code-reviewer, explorer, and the already-generalized planner) stay untouched with their current names — they ARE the code-mode perspectives. `framework/perspectives/` stays flat; base and code files coexist with distinct names. The base needs a MINIMUM VIABLE set of new perspective files — genuinely new names that aren't engineering-flavored variants. Start with the smallest set that lets Ralph function on a non-code PRD; more base roles can be added later. Existing prose in seed.md, ralph.md, planner.md, and prd.md (generalized in commit aa7a43c) must be reviewed against a 'move specifics, don't over-generalize' bar — roll back any paraphrase that hollowed out solid language; keep only the moves where code-specific content truly belonged elsewhere. That code-specific content (Green Builds, Code Cleaning, TDD verification sharpening, pipeline patterns, AGENTS.md paragraph, atomic commits sharpening, build-cycle.md) all lands in `framework/modes/code/MODE.md` — a single file per mode that acts as both (a) the code-specific PRD process addendum and (b) the registry of which perspectives this mode uses by name. Base `prd.md` gets one pointer sentence: 'If the PRD declares a mode field, also load modes/<mode>/MODE.md'. Base planner enumerates base roles explicitly (no directory scanning). IMPORTANT clarifications: code-cleaner and qa-engineer are NOT the same role — code-cleaner executes fixes directly (no feedback loop), qa-engineer evaluates from end-user perspective. Keep them distinct in all documentation. Architect chooses base role names. Produce a short manifest for task 002 naming every content block that lands in MODE.md. See orca-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/001/context.md for original audit.
tasks.outcome: Base has a minimum-viable set of new perspective files with architect-chosen neutral names. Existing perspective files are byte-identical and their names unchanged — they are the code-mode perspectives. Base prose is lean but not hollowed: over-generalizations from aa7a43c are rolled back; true specifics have moved. A short manifest tells task 002 what content lands in modes/code/MODE.md.
tasks.verification: Base perspective files make sense for a non-code domain without being contrived. Existing perspective files are byte-identical to their pre-task state (git diff against aa7a43c is empty for those). Base prose retains the punch and concreteness of the pre-aa7a43c version where the content was truly general. Role discovery path is explicit. Manifest is precise enough that task 002's architect can plan content moves without re-deriving boundaries.

tasks.description: Create the 'code' mode. Task 001 set up the shape: `framework/perspectives/` stays flat (all perspective files live there with their current names); base uses planner + drafter; code-specific content was extracted from base prose. Task 002 lands the mode itself — create `framework/modes/code/MODE.md` as a single file that (a) holds all the code-specific PRD process content extracted in task 001 (Green Builds, Code Cleaning, TDD verification sharpening, pipeline patterns, AGENTS.md paragraph, atomic commits sharpening, content from `framework/processes/build-cycle.md`) and (b) lists by filename the perspectives this mode uses from `framework/perspectives/`. Fold `framework/processes/build-cycle.md` into MODE.md and delete the original file. Add a `mode` field to `framework/templates/prd.json`. Wire planner/worker loading so MODE.md is read when the PRD declares a mode — the mechanism is already described in base prd.md's one-sentence pointer added in task 001, so task 002 ensures agents follow that pointer. Creating a new mode must be: add a `framework/modes/<name>/MODE.md` file; no framework code changes. A PRD with no mode works with only base roles. See `orca-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/001/design.md` §5 for the manifest of content blocks that must land in MODE.md.
tasks.outcome: `framework/modes/code/MODE.md` exists and contains all code-specific content extracted from base. `framework/processes/build-cycle.md` is gone (content folded in). `framework/templates/prd.json` has a `mode` field. A code PRD with `mode: "code"` produces identical behavior to before this PRD started. A PRD with no mode runs using base-only.
tasks.verification: An existing code PRD with `mode: "code"` runs through its pipeline and produces the same results it would have pre-PRD. A PRD with no mode field picks only base roles in its pipeline composition. Someone can create a 'research' mode by adding one file (`framework/modes/research/MODE.md`) without touching any other framework file. No content listed in task 001's §5 manifest is lost.

tasks.description: Multi-session support. Currently, `ralph-start.sh` from a second terminal intercepts the existing Docker session (`docker start -ai` reattaches). And two `ralph-loop.sh` instances on the same worktree cause git conflicts. Users need 2+ concurrent Ralph sessions against the same project — e.g., parallel PRDs. The Docker terminal problem is trivially solved with `docker exec`. The git isolation problem needs worktrees or clones so each session has its own working tree. The parallel-agents research doc (orca-context/designs/parallel-agents-research.md) has prior analysis — full clones recommended for Docker, worktrees for local. The solution should be simple enough to explain in one paragraph.
tasks.outcome: A user can launch a second Ralph session against the same project without disrupting the first. Each session has its own git working tree. The workflow is documented and easy.
tasks.verification: Start two sessions against the same project. Run different PRDs in each. Both complete without git conflicts or Docker session hijacking. The setup took under 2 minutes and no special knowledge.

tasks.description: Non-blocking question mechanism. When a subagent hits genuine ambiguity about intent or requirements that it can't resolve from context, it should be able to ask the human — without blocking other tasks. The question should be written to the task's context directory so it persists. Ralph should park that task and keep dispatching everything else. When Ralph runs out of dispatchable work (everything is complete, blocked, or waiting on answers), it surfaces all accumulated questions to the human in batch. The human answers, Ralph writes answers to task context, and re-dispatches those tasks. This must be opt-in per PRD — sometimes the human wants fully autonomous execution with no questions. A PRD that disallows questions means agents must make their best judgment call or block. See orca-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/004/context.md for mechanism brainstorming and open questions.
tasks.outcome: Agents can ask questions that don't stall the pipeline. Questions accumulate while other work continues. The human answers in batch when Ralph has nothing else to dispatch. PRDs control whether questions are allowed.
tasks.verification: Run a PRD with questions enabled. One task asks a question. Other tasks continue to completion. Ralph surfaces the question only after exhausting other work. Answer it, task resumes. Run a PRD with questions disabled. Agent that would have asked instead makes a judgment call and continues.

tasks.description: Rename the project from 'ralph-docker' to 'orca'. The `/ralph` skill name and prose references to the ralph loop/process stay. Everything else that's infrastructure/machinery-flavored becomes 'orca': `ralph-context/` → `orca-context/`, `.ralph/` → `.orca/`, infra scripts `ralph-{start,loop,once,reset,clone}.sh` → `orca-*.sh`, `RALPH_PROMPT.md` → `ORCA_PROMPT.md`, Docker image tag `ralph-claude` → `orca-claude`. Also delete `.ralph-tasks/` entirely (ghost directory — nothing in `framework/` or `.claude/skills/` references it; everything writes to `ralph-context/tasks/`). Handle `ralph-logs/` based on whether bash-loop mode survives. This repo is self-hosted so it's also its own first upgrade case. See orca-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/005/context.md for the full inventory.
tasks.outcome: Repo uses 'orca' for infrastructure/machinery and 'ralph' only for the loop process and `/ralph` skill name. Self-hosting still works end-to-end after the rename. README, CLAUDE.md, and docs are updated to match.
tasks.verification: `grep -r ralph` returns only intentional hits: process prose, `/ralph` skill path, `ralph-loop.sh` references that are actually describing the loop process. All infra scripts, dotfile dirs, log dirs, prompt file, and Docker image tag use orca. The framework still installs cleanly into itself and a `/ralph` run of an existing PRD works.

tasks.description: Make Orca easy to install into any project. Vision: from a fresh or existing project, the user tells Claude 'install Orca from <github URL>' and a skill fetches the framework and lays down all files, configs, and scaffolds — no manual steps, no separate installer script run. Re-running the install is safe: it upgrades the framework files, leaves user content alone, and migrates pre-rename installs (projects with `.ralph/`, `ralph-context/`, etc.) to the new orca-* paths. Includes git hook wiring, `.gitignore` additions, applying the recommended Claude settings template (see task 007), and `orca-context/` scaffolding. This task is on the larger side — architect should consider splitting during pipeline composition if the chosen mechanism expands scope (e.g., fresh install as one task, upgrade + rename migration as a follow-up). See orca-context/tasks/006-modes-and-multisession/006/context.md for mechanism options, upgrade/migration strategy, and open questions.
tasks.outcome: A user can install Orca into any git repo via a single instruction inside Claude Code, using current (April 2026) skill-install patterns. Re-running safely upgrades framework files. Re-running against a pre-rename `ralph-*` project migrates it to `orca-*` without losing user content. The existing `install.sh` is deleted.
tasks.verification: Install into a fresh empty git repo — all framework files, skills, settings, scaffolds, hooks, and gitignore entries are in place. Install into a pre-existing `ralph-*` installation — it migrates cleanly to `orca-*` with no lost user content. Install into an already-up-to-date project — it's a no-op with a clear report. The whole install takes under 30 seconds and requires no shell commands from the user.

tasks.description: Move the Claude Code settings template to a well-organized spot in `framework/` (currently parked at `framework/template.claude.settings.json`, should probably join `framework/templates/prd.json`). Wire the install skill to offer the recommendation, let the user pick destination (project vs user level) and whether to apply, and honor the choice. Not mandatory, never silent.
tasks.outcome: Settings template lives in a sensible location under `framework/`. Install skill offers the settings with context, user picks destination + apply/skip, outcome matches their choice.
tasks.verification: Install offers settings, user sees what they're accepting, declining leaves no trace, accepting lands them where the user chose without clobbering existing values.

tasks.description: Final cleanup after the rename (005), the new install flow (006), and settings move (007) have landed. Sweep the repo for any remaining stale references, ghost concepts, or documentation that contradicts the new state: README.md gets a full rewrite to match the Orca identity and current install story; CLAUDE.md updated for renamed paths and removed concepts (`.ralph-tasks/`, maybe `ralph-logs/`); `docs/structure.md` and other docs updated; stale references in `ralph-context/tasks/` (old PRD context files) flagged or left alone based on their historical value. Also remove anything else the preceding tasks didn't already clean up.
tasks.outcome: The repo reads as a coherent 'Orca' project. README accurately describes what Orca is, how to install it, and how to use it. No docs reference paths or concepts that no longer exist. No ghost directories mentioned in guidance.
tasks.verification: Read the README end-to-end: every install step, path, and concept matches reality. `grep -r ralph` returns only intentional hits (process prose, `/ralph` skill path). No references to `.ralph-tasks/` anywhere in current docs. A newcomer following the README can install and use Orca without hitting stale instructions.
